operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+pln-config.scm and scheme load path (14f46800437264de)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: pln-config.scm and scheme load path
Messages: 2
Date range: 2015-08-19 to 2015-08-20
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14f46800437264de

============================================================

--- Message 1/2 ---
From: Eddie Monroe
Date: 2015-08-19

Hi Nil, how's it going?



From the cogserver scheme shell, when I run:

    guile> (load "../opencog/reasoning/pln/pln-config.scm"



I get:

    ERROR: In procedure primitive-load-path: Unable to find file

"utilities.scm" in load path



Manually adding the needed directory to the load path fixes the problem:

    guile>(add-to-load-path "/usr/local/share/opencog/scm")



But I'm wondering if we want this added automatically somewhere in the

cogserver or scheme startup scripts.





In the pln-config.scm file, there is

    (use-modules (opencog))



and atomspace/opencog/scm/opencog.scm contains:

    (add-to-load-path "/usr/local/share/opencog/scm")

            and

    (define-module (opencog))



but the add-to-path comes before the define-module (not sure if that

matters),



 [...]

--- Message 2/2 ---
From: 'Nil Geisweiller' via opencog
Date: 2015-08-20

Hi Eddie!



this is related to the last 3 comments of this PR



https://github.com/opencog/opencog/pull/1670



scheme file loading is still a mystery to me. I think there is a flaw in 

scheme itself that doesn't not update the current path when a file is 

loaded, it considers an include, just dump the content in the caller and 

doesn't update its current path global variable.



I'll investigate soon.



Nil

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+PLN tasks (14d47ddfe72295eb)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: PLN tasks
Messages: 8
Date range: 2015-05-12 to 2015-05-14
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14d47ddfe72295eb

============================================================

--- Message 1/8 ---
From: Nil Geisweiller
Date: 2015-05-12

Im ready to resume work on PLN and Id like to get an idea of who is doing what. Amen is adding PLN rules, Misgana is working on ECAN + experimenting with forward chainer, William is refactoring the URE code. It seems the biggest overlapping would be between William and I. For now Im merely gonna improve guile [non-cogserver] usages of the URE example + get rid of JSON as config file.

--- Message 2/8 ---
From: Amen Belayneh
Date: 2015-05-12

Nope, I am just assisting Prateek (as everyone is) in porting the rules.

--- Message 3/8 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-05-12

I recently implemented AddLink and RemoveLink and AssignLink which might be useful for PLN, or might not be. I plan to rename AddLink to InsertLink later today, to make it clear that it is inserting into the atomspace, rather than adding numbers.

--- Message 4/8 ---
From: William Ma
Date: 2015-05-12

My most immediate task is change the Backward Chainers stopping criteria. The do_full_chain method will most likely be removed since I think it will be impossible to tell if the Backward Chainer is done after this change. It is, however, more aligned with the wiki design. [...]

--- Message 5/8 ---
From: Nil Geisweiller
Date: 2015-05-13

You could also name it AddAtomLink. InsertLink may sound like an operator that would insert an atom in a SubSetLink. Anyway, as long the name is well documented I guess it matters little.

--- Message 6/8 ---
From: Eddie Monroe
Date: 2015-05-13

I think the forward chainer is going to need some work. Nil, perhaps we can chat early in the day tomorrow my time, later in the day your time.

--- Message 7/8 ---
From: Misgana Bayetta
Date: 2015-05-14

Pull the latest atomspace and it works. If you have new features in mind or problems while running, It will be helpful to create issues on github.

--- Message 8/8 ---
From: Eddie Monroe
Date: 2015-05-14

Misgana, I believe there are still some issues. With the latest atomspace code a single inference step on a single submitted source took 12 hours for an atomspace of ~10K atoms. Lets chat offline about the details.

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+PLN to-do list (14c2315e5a55ad4c)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: PLN to-do list
Messages: 3
Date range: 2015-03-16 to 2015-03-17
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14c2315e5a55ad4c

============================================================

--- Message 1/3 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-03-16

Hi William, Here are TODO items in the PLN universe: 1) Make PLN handle the full variety of inference rules, 2) Implement a more clever Rule of Choice, 3) Take a careful look at inference trails, 4) Implement a fancier revision rule, 5) Make PLN deal with EmbeddedTruthValueLink, 6) Make IndefiniteTruthValue formulas efficient, 7) Implement the New ANDRule for PLN, 8) Implement the optimal universe size formula, 9) Implement confidence decay.

-- Ben

--- Message 2/3 ---
From: Amen Belayneh
Date: 2015-03-17

It would be great if the truthvalue types of the different atoms are accounted for in the TV calculations.

--- Message 3/3 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-03-17

Two things I was forgetting: Average Quantifier, and treating links with unquantified embedded variables as implicit Predicate Nodes. Also Boolean operators involving ConceptNodes.

Matt, perhaps you could boil down my list into a series of GitHub Issues?

ben

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+PPL in Atomese (150c6df4ea8551be)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: PPL in Atomese
Messages: 8
Date range: 2015-11-02 to 2015-11-04
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 150c6df4ea8551be

============================================================

--- Message 1/8 ---
From: 'Nil Geisweiller' via opencog
Date: 2015-11-02

Seeing those good ideas floating around gave me the extra motivation I needed to write about this one (I have had it in the back of my mind for months) https://github.com/opencog/opencog/issues/1846

--- Message 2/8 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-11-02

Isnt what we really want: Probability distributions over the Atomspace? I think PLN inference control would be a perfect first use-case for PPL on the Atomspace.... An inference trajectory can be thought of as a sample from a space of possible inference trajectories. The inference history thats been stored, is a prior distribution biasing this sampling process... Maybe you can turn to this after you implement type signatures, which will be after you finish fixing the URE ...

--- Message 3/8 ---
From: 'Nil Geisweiller' via opencog
Date: 2015-11-02

Indeed, if you mean by that upgrade Atomese (or its standard library) to support any probability distributions. Oh! do you mean that if for instance the inference control were encoded as an Atomese program, then one could use PPL techniques to query the probability of this or that part of the control program to be in this or that state, conditioned on inference success? That way we could use PPL to build meta-knowledge about the inference control and improve it!

--- Message 4/8 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-11-02

Yes, and then we will want helper functions to choose Atoms according to specified distributions over the Atomspace... Yes... we had planned to do this anyway but PPL gives a different way to wrap it and think about it... Shujings pattern miner can be used for mining patterns in inference histories, but this can be viewed from a PPL perspective as a means of estimating distributions...

--- Message 5/8 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-11-03

Yes. Here is what I have had in the back of my mind, for a while, but havent done much, yet: implement a measure-valued version of AndLink, OrLink that actually did set sizes (probability) correctly (i.e. set-intersection and set-union). Use ContextLink to indicate conditional probabilities, so that P(B|A) would be ContextLink A B. Have the above work in some simple, self-consistent manner, so that anyone could do basic bayesian or markovian probability in the atomspace. I think it would be cool to have this... Ideally, we could have some HMM demo.

--- Message 6/8 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-11-03

In PLN lingo, those would be called direct evaluation rules for AndLink and OrLink... The idea of ContextLink is that if we have ContextLink A BlahBlah where BlahBlah is a complex structure, then all the probabilities in the Atoms inside BlahBlah are conditioned on A... This is cool. But it doesnt really give full-on probabilistic programming for OpenCog. Im background-reading some papers on prob. prog.... Alexey Potapov is eager to collaborate on this stuff next year...

--- Message 7/8 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-11-04

Well, yes, but popular textbook algorithms should be easy to implement in opencog. It would help with general acceptance of the framework. To quote perls motto: simple things should be easy, and hard things should not be impossible. I have not yet understood why I should get excited about prob prog... what makes it better than bayesian nets or markov nets? higher level of abstraction? In what way, exactly, does probabilistic lisp differ from e.g. a markov net?

--- Message 8/8 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-11-04

As for why PPL goes beyond MLN, the basic issues with MLN are that the representation language is too simplistic and the inference algorithm is too inefficient.... MLN is an example of PPL, just not a powerful example... One core idea is that a good way to MODEL a set of data, is often: to figure out a way to GENERATE that data. But sometimes data is messy and noisy and is best viewed as the result of some complex, structured yet stochastic process. The inference problem then arises: What distributions on the parameters will cause a certain probabilistic program P to generate the observed data? Another interesting application would be action as inverse perception. Potapov has used simulated annealing and GP for this purpose.

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Problems about cython binding (14d906901d59ae3c)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Problems about cython binding
Messages: 6
Date range: 26 May 2015 to 28 May 2015
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14d906901d59ae3c

============================================================

--- Message 1/6 ---
From: Yi-Shan Chen
Date: 26 May 2015

Hi,

Recently I'm building the cython binding of SpaceServer, and I meet some problems.
I cdef a class PySpaceServer and want to call the c++ function inside the member function.
But the compiler complained: 'Handle' is not a type identifier.
 [...]

--- Message 2/6 ---
From: Yi-Shan Chen
Date: 26 May 2015

Oh, I put the __init__.pyx in the /usr/local/include/opencog/cython/opencog and cimport them successfully.

--- Message 3/6 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-05-27

There already is a copy of __init__.py in /usr/local/share/opencog/python/opencog/__init__.py  I don't understand why a second copy is needed in /usr/local/include

--linas

--- Message 4/6 ---
From: Yi-Shan Chen
Date: 27 May 2015

Hi linas,

That's because I'd like to "from opencog.atomspace cimport CHandler, cAtomSpace,Handle,AtomSpace"
So I must include atomspace.pxd.
For cimport, cython only search .pxd file under the path in the -I cython compiler option.

--- Message 5/6 ---
From: Yi-Shan Chen
Date: 27 May 2015

Oops, sorry for typo, I missed "local" in the both paths in the previous post.

--- Message 6/6 ---
From: DongMin Kim
Date: 28 May 2015

Hi linas and YiShan,

I agree with YiShan's opinion that the __init__.py file in /usr/local/include is needed by out-of-atomspace project.
The '.so' files are cythonized libraries required at execute time.
However, an 'atomspace.pxd' file acts like 'C header file', required at compile time.
 [...]

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Problem building Cogserver, handle atoms_eq (1507320df4aee9c7)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Problem building Cogserver, handle atoms_eq
Messages: 6
Date range: 2015-10-16 to 2015-10-18
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 1507320df4aee9c7

============================================================

--- Message 1/6 ---
From: Kino Coursey
Date: 2015-10-16

Anyone have an idea about the following build error? libserver.so: undefined reference to opencog::Handle::atoms_eq. I just did a git pull and build on cogutil, atomspace and opencog.

--- Message 2/6 ---
From: Amen Belayneh
Date: 2015-10-17

Have you installed atomspace and cogutils and run sudo ldconfig if you are on a debian family system?

--- Message 3/6 ---
From: Kino Coursey
Date: 2015-10-17

Yes to all of the above (it is Ubuntu-14.10 in a VM); same results.

--- Message 4/6 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-10-18

For the atomspace you need to make clean first. I recently changed the signature of that function. CMake should have realized this and automatically rebuilt everything, but apparently it did not.

--- Message 5/6 ---
From: Kino Coursey
Date: 2015-10-18

Thanks Linas! make clean solved the problem. While an extra usually not necessary step, it may be useful to add to the noob recipe.

--- Message 6/6 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-10-18

Yeah, usually the infrastructure like this does not change too often, but this one gave a 5% or 10% performance boost.

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+projects for GSoC 2015 (14ad8457679bf71f)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: projects for GSoC 2015
Messages: 6
Date range: 2015-01-04 to 2015-01-14
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14ad8457679bf71f

============================================================

--- Message 1/6 ---
From: Amartya Sanyal
Date: 2015-01-04

I am willing to do a project under OpenCog in GSoC 2015. I have done work in Machine Learning, face recognition, neural networks.

--- Message 2/6 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-01-11

The opencog wiki has a page called Ideas which lists various things. Re: face recognition: what, currently, is the best open-source library for face detection and face recognition in live video? Any chance that its wrapped up in a ROS node?

--- Message 3/6 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-01-11

I would be very happy to supervise a project using and further developing Py-DeSTIN for object recognition, or face recognition. Extending the frequent subtree mining on DeSTIN trees code that Ted Sanders wrote last year to pyDeSTIN interests me particularly.

--- Message 4/6 ---
From: Amartya Sanyal
Date: 2015-01-14

I was actually thinking in terms of hybrid networks. Something that is highly customizable and can be adapted according to the task. I have experience with Convolutional Neural Networks.

--- Message 5/6 ---
From: Amartya Sanyal
Date: 2015-01-14

Using DeSTIN and SVM or Neural Nets to Effectively Classify Images is one that certainly interests me.

--- Message 6/6 ---
From: Amartya Sanyal
Date: 2015-01-14

We can actually include the feature extraction mechanism within DeSTIN so that it can work on raw data. Convolutional Neural Nets perform this task gracefully.

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Proposal on semantic context based exploration for robots (1505a9a93dcbe97f)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Proposal on semantic context based exploration for robots
Messages: 2
Date range: 11 Oct 2015 to 2015-10-13
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 1505a9a93dcbe97f

============================================================

--- Message 1/2 ---
From: Tanmay Shankar
Date: 11 Oct 2015

Hello, My name is Tanmay, I'm an undergrad at IIT Guwahati. For my Bachelors thesis project, I am working on providing robots with a semantic context based understanding of their environment. I am interested in knowing whether it would be a valuable contribution to OpenCog.

--- Message 2/2 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-10-13

Hi, At the high level, this idea makes reasonable sense. OpenCog as a framework is pretty flexible. Whether what you implement ends up generally useful will depend on the particular architecture you choose. If you do your work in close discussion with the community you'll be more likely to 'get it right'.

Welcome ;-)
Ben

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Putting deep learning perception into OpenCog (150c26368a01877f)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Putting deep learning perception into OpenCog
Messages: 5
Date range: 2015-11-01 to 2015-11-03
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 150c26368a01877f

============================================================

--- Message 1/5 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-11-01

Some general thoughts on the blog. A fairly specific design suggestion on the wiki page Deep Learning Perception in OpenCog.

--- Message 2/5 ---
From: 'Nil Geisweiller' via hk-addis@opencog.org
Date: 2015-11-02

Although I can't comment on the specifics as I don't have much experience with deep learning, I think this is an excellent suggestion.

--- Message 3/5 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-11-02

I have been supervising students and some iCog staff experimenting with deep learning vision for a few years now, so I think I have a good sense of the area, although it hasn't been used in OpenCog yet...

--- Message 4/5 ---
From: Jim Rutt
Date: 2015-11-02

Seems to me the Cognitive Synergy aspect would be if broad high level inferences and probabilities from the atomspace could be continuously feedback into the deep learning implementation. [...]

--- Message 5/5 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-11-03

Yeah, that will be the big win from an AGI perspective. Along the way, we will also be creating the most flexible tool for building deep learning architectures out there.

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Python execution in EvaluationLinkdo_evaluate 'not implemented' (14bc1218c37c175a)

--linas

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+question on using the full_reduct function (14f2033a702059b4)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: question on using the full_reduct function
Messages: 8
Date range: 2015-08-11 to 2015-08-19
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14f2033a702059b4

============================================================

--- Message 1/8 ---
From: anticancer8108@gmail.com
Date: 2015-08-11

A question about the use of MOSES. I went through the code of moses and did a few attempts at applying it (e.g. regression modelling of a data table). One thing I noted is that it appears to just create a combo tree that is boolean or real-valued. [...]

--- Message 2/8 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-08-12

Your input table should have a mix of boolean and continuous-valued columns in it. Note that moses has never worked particularly well for continuous-valued inputs; this may change after the PSO optimization is added this summer, for the GSOC project. Note that, in general, most continuous-valued inputs can be very noisy, and that binning it into discrete (boolean) bins is an excellent way of filtering out noise.

--- Message 3/8 ---
From: anticancer8108@gmail.com
Date: 2015-08-13

Could you briefly explain what you mean by moses never worked well with continuous variables? Does it mean that it is unreliable at spawning a combo program that gives good regression on a continuous data table, or does it just mean that higher numbers of iterations is needed? I remember that due to [...]

--- Message 4/8 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-08-14

It took too long to get a good answer -- sometimes, even a cpu-day was not enough to get a good answer. Not the principles, but the implementation. The way it explored the space of float point values was inefficient (for most problems). We are fixing this with the PSO code. Exploring a space of float-point values is a fine art to itself, there are many books describing many algorithms, each with strengths and weaknesses...

--- Message 5/8 ---
From: anticancer8108@gmail.com
Date: 2015-08-15

I noticed that the subtree_to_enf() is marked out (deactivated) in full_reduction.cc. This is the function for reduction of boolean trees into elegant normal form. [...]

--- Message 6/8 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-08-16

Dont know the answers to any of the questions below. You are welcome to uncomment it, and see what happens. To see its effect, be sure to 1) run with 12 or 20 different initial random seeds 2) track the highest score achieved for each seed 3) measure how long each run took. Trying several different datasets would be good too.

--- Message 7/8 ---
From: anticancer8108@gmail.com
Date: 2015-08-17

okay i got it, thanks for everything. will take some time to try out and mull over everything

--- Message 8/8 ---
From: 'Nil Geisweiller' via opencog
Date: 2015-08-19

I dont recall well as its been a while, I think its just cause subtree_to_enf hasnt been implemented to work on mixed trees, only boolean trees. Im sure it wouldnt be difficult to make it work on mixed trees, well once you understand it.

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Re-opening discussion on an old Relex2Logic issue (14d4c3f7c123e304)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Re-opening discussion on an old Relex2Logic issue
Messages: 13
Date range: 2015-05-13 to 15 May 2015
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14d4c3f7c123e304

============================================================

--- Message 1/13 ---
From: Aaron Nitzkin
Date: 2015-05-13

I am currently in the midst of translating all the old relex2logic rules
into atomese and one issue conitnually comes up that I think we discussed a
little bit when I wrote the question rules last fall . . . I am continually
finding that the way we are doing this demands that I write many times the
 [...]

--- Message 2/13 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-05-13

Yes it would be better to write postprocessing code to handle this...

--- Message 3/13 ---
From: Aaron Nitzkin
Date: 2015-05-13

Just in case, not everybody realizes . . . the reason that one can't simply
modify the constituent within a normal SVO predicate structure for example,
is that the entire predicate-argument structure (currently) is within a
satisfyingsetlink, so that you are instructing PLN to identify "which
 [...]

--- Message 4/13 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-05-13

Absolutely right.

Do it, Aaron.

Didn't  I tell you so?

So, when I recanted "triples", you said "bully for you", but apparently
neither you nor anyone else understood what i was talking about.  And so
R2L re-invented triples all over again, but under a different name. So it
goes.

But now it seems that you rediscovered the problem. And a solution!  And,
amazingly the solution is ... wait for it ... stay close to the original
dependency parse!  Wow!

There is a technical/theoretical reason for this. Please excuse me in
advance, but the point is important, and you are at exactly the stage where
you can glimpse why.

You observe that you need 9 rules, when one should have been enough. This
is commonly, historically called a  "counting argument", and is the
 [...]

--- Message 5/13 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-05-13

Heh.

And one more point: so when I say "choices", this is a synonym for
"disjuncts": the choices are disjoined, the entropy is the log of the
number of disjuncts or choices.

For you the 9 rules you apply to handle SV, SVO, SVIO etc these rules are
all "disjoined" -- you apply one rule or another but not all at the same
time.

Thus, the thing to count is not really "the number of rules" but "the
number of disjoined rules".  This is important: rules that are not
disjoined do not increase the entropy.

A linear chain (A implies B implies C) has an entropy of zero, because the
walk, the path from A to B to C was unique.  You have no choices, no
alternatives.  If these are relex rules, you apply A then B then C. If
these are PLN rules, the same.

 [...]

--- Message 6/13 ---
From: Aaron Nitzkin
Date: 2015-05-14

On Wed, May 13, 2015 at 6:21 PM, Linas Vepstas <linasvepstas@gmail.com>
wrote:

Well, although the coming discussion is wonderfully philosophical, I have
to say, that 1) it's hardly a massive insight to notice that one
subject-rule would be better than nine; I think most 12-year olds would
 [...]

--- Message 7/13 ---
From: Aaron Nitzkin
Date: 2015-05-14

each one embodies a different post-verbal complement, but when you multiply
them by other dimensions of variation, they become redundant because you
want to take an SVO question, and process it by recognizing that it is SVO
AND a question, not an SVO-question, which requires there be another rule
 [...]

--- Message 8/13 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-05-14

Hi Aaron,

Well, its obvious when the system is small, but  becomes very hard once all
the possibilities don't easily fit in your short-term memory.

2)  It's also known a little less esoterically as the Principle of Least

Whack.

No, that's something different. That gives you geodesics on symplectic
manifolds; it is not clear (to me) what the manifold is (if any) in the
present case.  There is the idea of "information geometry", for which the
kullback-liebler divergence is the local metric.  But its not at all clear
(to me) how this applies, if at all, to these various cases.

I guess. The entropy is a logarithm of the disorder.  There are 256
different bytes, but the entropy  of a byte is 8.

No. Action is defied only on symplectic manifolds. Because tangent
 [...]

--- Message 9/13 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-05-14

Continuing, in a more concrete direction.

The word commonly used in such cases is "orthogonality": one says that this
set is "orthogonal" to that set, if what I do here does not affect what
happens there.  Sometimes one says that the two sets are "independent" of
one-another.

A property of orthogonality is that its multiplicative: if there are M
items in one set, and N in another, then, combinatorially, there are M
times N in the two combined.   Don't laugh. This can be subtle.

But how does one discover orthogonal sets? One can mess around, hope for
the best. For linear algebra, this was pounded flat in the 19th century.
For other cases, there has been progress.  For (simple) statistical data,
textbook Bayesian probability provides tools. For language data, we have no
tools. [...]

From: Aaron Nitzkin
Date: 2015-05-15

Hey Linas,

2)  It's also known a little less esoterically as the Principle of Least

Whack.

manifolds; it is not clear (to me) what the manifold is (if any) in the
present case. [...]

From: Linas Vepstas

Let me know when you meet a scientist who claims to understand what they're

And perhaps that is why mathematicians never know what they are talking
about?  Only crackpots will tell you how certain they are.

I'm just trying to convey the reality of the situation.  When you meet a
professor emeritus in number theory who tells you that he doesn't know what
a number is, or doesn't understand addition, well, of course, they are
being coy. But they are are also being honest. Just because 10-year-olds
can perform addition, or that it was done thousands of years ago, does not
mean that addition is understandable or comprehensible by human beings.  To
this very day, number theory journals are packed with articles providing
 [. [...]

numbers together.

http://www.math.wayne.edu/~isaksen/Expository/carrying.pdf

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Recording global count values... (14d966f5cc9e4c7a)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Recording global count values...
Messages: 28
Date range: 2015-05-28 to 2015-05-31
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14d966f5cc9e4c7a

============================================================

--- Message 1/28 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-05-28

For NLP Atoms (among others), Man Hin and Amen and I were discussing
using some variant of CountTruthValue that *only* stores the count
value in the TV object

Then, if n= count, the other parameters of the truth value could be
calculated on the fly, e.g. by

strength = n / N

confidence = n / (n+k)

...

In this case the question is begged: what is N?

For an Atomspace entirely consisting of Atoms derived from text, if
the Atom in question is a WordNode, then N could be the total number
of word-instances observed by the system, i.e. N is the sum of the
counts of all WordInstanceNodes in the system

More broadly, N would be interpreted in PLN "experiential semantics"
as the "number of observations" made or inferred by the system...

 [...]

--- Message 2/28 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-05-27

Based on my poor recollection of readings in child psychology, human
children are exposed to approx 50 million words before age 8.  Assuming an
8-year-old knows 5K words, and a flat distribution, that means N=50M/5K =
10K   Of course, the distribution is Zipfian, not flat, so some words are
experiences a million times, some are experienced a few dozen times. Sorry,
I don't have a reference.

I've also read reports that categorical grammars, learned via some kind of
HMM-type device, when trained on a mother-child text corpus, take approx 1K
to 10K observations to get the "right" meanings for words.  However: the
researcher noted that there is a snowball effect or a domino effect: the
network moves through random states until it eventually correctly learns
 [...]

--- Message 3/28 ---
From: Nil Geisweiller
Date: 2015-05-28

Linas, It's all very interesting but it seems a bit off topic, right?

The question is more broad, how to keep track of the the sample size of 
the distribution being estimated.

I'm not sure. Perhaps each atom (each TV) should keep track of both n 
and N. But of course there's gonna be a lot of atoms with identical Ns, 
thus I understand Ben's suggestion to keep track of it globally.

My initial suggestion would be to store everything on ContextLinks

So if you've got

ContextLink
    ConceptNode C
    ConceptNode A

You'll know the N for the ContextLink is n for C (or it's cardinality or 
such).

I guess that would be the right, general, but also inefficient way to 
deal with it. To optimize that, maybe we'll realized we want to keep 
 [...]

--- Message 4/28 ---
From: Nil Geisweiller
Date: 2015-05-28

Sorry, I should have said the context itself (here C), not the context link.

Nil

--- Message 5/28 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-05-28

Nil,

Thanks for the suggestions!

***
 To optimize that, maybe we'll realized we want to keep track of them
globally, in some C++ AtomSpace structure, but clearly that should
only be used as an optimization trick.
***

Yes, this sounds correct.

Also, note this is an immediate need for the work Man Hin is doing....

Man Hin, William and I could work out a "hacky" specialized C++
AtomSpace structure that would serve our immediate need (of keeping
track of the total count of certain Atom types, specifically
WordInstanceNodes) -- but if you have a specific alternate suggestion

On Thu, May 28, 2015 at 5:52 PM, Nil Geisweiller
<ngeiswei@googlemail.com> wrote:

--- Message 7/28 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-05-28

I guess I don't understand the problem, or why you think you need the
capital-N device.  The way I understood it made it seem like a bad idea.

For example, just to stay grounded: The language-learning code keeps track
of counts. Then, periodically (every hour, every day, every week), scripts
will run that then sum up tens of thousands of different sub-totals and
correlations, and then does the divisions and the logarithms, etc.

The point here is that any one single count is almost totally useless--
what matters is how the different counts relate to one-another: their
relative values (differences, ratios, etc).  It is not important how often
the word X was observed -- its only important how often the word X was
observed, compared to word Y.

 [...]

--- Message 8/28 ---
From: Nil Geisweiller
Date: 2015-05-28

Ben, etc,

Having thought a bit deeper, I stand by what I've initially suggested, 
that is using the count on the context node as N, and work with the 
ContextLink TV.

However, I don't understand in details what you want to accomplish with 
NLP (for reasoning about that kind of problem I usually imagine sensory 
data streams and patterns of different time scales, therefore with 
different Ns). But you seem to be saying that you could get away with 
this just counting the number of atoms of a certain type, right?

If so, you've got this method in AtomSpace

     inline int getNumAtomsOfType(Type type, bool subclass = false) const
         { return atomTable.getNumAtomsOfType(type, subclass); }

having looked at TypeIndex::getNumAtomsOfType, it would clearly not be 
 [...]

From: Amen Belayneh
Date: 28 May 2015

Sentence1: This is whatever
Sentehnce2: This is another whatever 

n-of-this = 2
n-of-is = 2
n-of-another= 1
n-of-whatever=1

N as described by Ben, is merely the sum of the above. So having an 
autoupdated N for the single contex of "all language ever inputed to the 
atomspace" is what is being proposed. As Nil mentioned this should be 
wrapped in a ContextLink, when dealing with mulitple context, as far as I 
know we are not, and all the atoms in the atomspace are within the same

The problem of identfying a context, keeping track of data relevant to that

From: Ben Goertzel

Hi guys,

It seems we are having two different discussions here

Of course these are interrelated.  But it's also worth thinking about

Generally, we want OpenCog to provide tools to support multiple
different AI methodologies.

I agree (with Nil and Linas) that most of the time, in NLP or
reasoning, the number we want to calculate isn't going to be

count of Atom A
---------------------
total count of all Atoms of type T (the type of Atom A)

Most of the time we'll want to calculate frequencies within a narrower
context....

On the other hand, right now, for relatively small-scale NLP
experiments, in my view it would be pretty convenient to be able to do
 [...]

From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-05-28

Hi Amen,

(Hi Rohit, Ainish)

The current learning code uses a special atom type called AnyNode for
holding summed-up counts.

Thus, for example

(EvaluationLink  <TV1>
  (DefinedLinguisticRelationshipNode "_subj")
  (ListLink
     (WordInstanceNode "is")
     (AnyNode "second word of subject"   <tv2>)
  )

is used to hold the counts for  sum over all words $x such that

(EvaluationLink
  (DefinedLinguisticRelationshipNode "_subj")
  (ListLink
     (WordInstanceNode "is")
     (VariableNode "$x")
  )

I don't remember if the counts are stored in tv1 or in tv2. I think they're
stored in tv1, but I'm not sure as I write this email.

I originally used the pattern matcher to find and total these counts, but
discovered that was rather slow (at least it was several years ago, it
 [...]

--- Message 13/28 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-05-28

I'm going to repeat my email to Amen, slightly,

So,  right now, the way the code currently works is that it calculates the
ratio:

count of Atom A
---------------------
total count of all Atoms of type T in a certain "shape"

An example "shape" is the below:

(EvaluationLink  <TV>
  (DefinedLinguisticRelationshipNode "_subj")
  (ListLink
     (WordInstanceNode "is")
     (AnyNode "second word of subject")
  )

The above ratio is stored in TV (the current code actually stores minus the
log of that ratio in the "confidence" field. That's because the numeric
value of the ratio is typically  0.000001 and its hard on the eyes to count
all those zeros.)

Please recall that Rohit's and Ainish's  GSOC summer project for this year
 [...]

--- Message 14/28 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-05-29

Hi,

OK, yeah, this is quite close to what we want....   However, I wonder
what process keeps the TV of this "shape" link updated...

Hmm, if I understand correctly, that's a bit odd because the TV of
that shape is likely to change over time, right?  So if the ratio is
stored in the TV of the Atom, then how is it updated when the TV of
the "shape" Atom changes...?

Sure, whether it's stored in log form or not is not important.
However, I would prefer you came up with a different name than
"confidence" for that number, just because it's quite different than
what PLN means by confidence, and hence likely to cause confusion....

In fact this is very close to what PLN calls *strength* not what PLN
calls confidence.  In PLN language, this value is (the log of) a
 [...]

--- Message 15/28 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-05-29

It is a two-step process.  Raw counts for words and link-grammar links are
updated with each new sentence. Counts for relex relations are not
maintained, they are ignored.  On a less frequent basis, the ratios and
derived quantities are computed. A detailed guide is at the bottom of email.

Yes

The raw counts are updated with every new sentence. Then, infrequently, the
grand-total counts are computed, the ratios are computed, the TV for the
shape is updated.

You could do this updating with every new sentence, if you wish, but
there's a lot of it, so that is wasteful.  A given word like "the" or "is",
might occur in a thousand shapes: you don;'t really need to update a
thousand ratios (or more!!) just because you saw the word "is" one more
time.

However, see below.

Yeah, I know. [...]

--- Message 16/28 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-05-30

Thanks Linas... we will think this through further, and discuss Monday

I'll give a two part response --- one part about the count truth value
issue specifically, and another part about associating values with
Atoms generally...

1)
ABOUT COUNT TRUTH VALUES

Preliminarily, here is what Man Hin and I were discussing today...

We are thinking to introduce a SimpleCountTruthValue, which stores

Then other values derived from the count could be calculated on the fly...

We could have methods of the general nature

getStrength( optional-argument Context)
getConfidence( optional-argument Context, optional-argument k)

getLogStrength( optional-argument Context)
... etc.

 [...]

--- Message 17/28 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-05-29

If these are some random snippets of python code or some bogus scheme
utilities, I don't care.  If you are proposing changes to the AtomSpace API
(which I think you are) then it is a terrible idea. For many reasons:

1) it requires changes to the C++, scheme, python, haskell and REST API's.
That is five things you have to change, 5 API's you have to test.

2) you are inventing a new structure called "context". It needs to be
reference counted and made thread-safe. You will have to put locks in all
the right places, You will need complicated unit tests to test the locks.
The reference counting will have to place nice with the truth values,
 Setting the truth value on an atom is actually very complicated: first you
lock, then you copy, then you decrement the reference count on the old
 [...]

From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-05-30

Hi,

I'll split my reply into two separate threads to avoid (any further) confusion.

Reply #1 (this one) is about the issue of SimpleCountTV  ... the other

...

...

In this thread I am not proposing changes to the AtomSpace API.   In

You've spoken often about the values of prototypes.  I suspect when we

  Perhaps it's better for Man Hin and me to take that approach than to
have lengthy email discussions about it -- since, as noted, in the
 [...]

From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-05-30

OK, I'm confused about why we are discussing this, then.  People write all

project-specific directory where it doesn't impact anyone else, the general
rule is "anything goes".

Since I'm well aware that much of what I've recently been doing has in fact
been disruptive to others, I'm touchy on the subject.  As long as the new
code isn't disruptive, it can be designed in any way desired.  Mostly:
don't put it in the atomspace directory, don't monkey with the existing
structures in the atomspace directory, and life will be good.

Frankly, I have no clue why the proposed API is even remotely interesting
or useful, or what problems it solves. Nothing we've talked about so far
would appear to solve any problem I actually have. [...]

--- Message 20/28 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-05-30

However, I know you have a different preferred way of storing TV
information regarding NLP Atoms, and by now have I figured out it's
probably not a good use of our time to argue the point... we can
 [...]

--- Message 21/28 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-05-30

What you seem to be thinking is that it is better update the TV on fifty

Yes!

Yes!

No!  You can't obtain the normalization for a single TV without having to
touch 50K or 100K or even more atoms to obtain the required counts. You
cannot do this "on the fly".  Well, you can, but in that case, it will take
 [...]

--- Message 22/28 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-05-30

Hi,

Consider a very simple example.

Suppose we want, for a specific Atom A, to be able to calculate the ratio

s = n / N

n =  # times the entity referenced by A has occurred in the system's experience

N = # times any entity of the same sort as A has occurred in the
system's experience

For instance, if A is the WordNode "cheese", then we might have

n = # times the word "cheese" has occurred in texts the system has read

N = # distinct word-instances that have occurred in texts the system has read

I realize this is not always the most useful quantity to calculate,
but I'm just giving a simple example for illustration...

What I am suggesting is

-- the TruthValue for A contains the number n

-- a global variable associated w/ the Atomspace maintains the number N

 [...]

--- Message 23/28 ---
From: Matthew Kaufman
Date: 2015-05-30

In regard to doing 50K to 100K counts on the fly; can this API you're
talking about utilize Redis?  It is a highly fast key/value store.  Super
fast.

It would work easily.... Not 5 minutes per sentence... I think.... API
development is my specialty, language and link grammar is an interest...
 [... [...]

--- Message 24/28 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-05-30

lets say that A is the single word is "cheese"

You are falsely assuming that there is only one single "entity of the same
sort as A". But there isn't. You are not observing the word "cheese" in
isolation; it is a part of a sentence.  Thus, there's going to be at least
two or three of the below:

_subj(cheese, *)
_obj (*, cheese)
_amod(*, cheese)
_det(*, cheese)

The star * here means "wildcard", it means "sum over the counts of all
things appearing in location *"  I've been using the wildcard AnyNode
(AnyWordNode) as that placeholder.

These wild-card sums appear as denominators in tens of thousands of
fractions:  for example:

P(amod(wisconsin, cheese)) = n(_amod(wisconsin, cheese)) / N(_amod(*,
cheese)) * N(_amod(wisconsin, *)) / N(_amod(*,*))

 [...]

--- Message 25/28 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-05-31

Hi,

YaHHH!!! -- now you got it ... that is what I was thinking of when I
was discussing w/ Man Hin in the office...

Right.   Batching is more efficient but it means the numbers are
always off, which may be OK, but if possible I'd prefer not to
introduce extra errors into already very messy sort of process...

That's what I was thinking...

I'm not sure either.  The point of my original email in this thread
was to solicit input from folks (including you) about what the API for
this should be like...

Whatever API is used, must obviously make it simple to specify what
shapes are interesting enough to be on-the-fly updated...

(Obviously, for other shapes, if their count values are needed, these
can be computed at the time; the difference is that for the
 [...]

--- Message 26/28 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-05-30

Hi Ben and Rohit and Ainish

OK, so here is what I know so far.  I hope that Rohit and Ainish are paying
attention, because the following will affect them, before the summer is
over.

If you observe a single word "cheese", (ignoring everything else) then the
probability is easy:

P(cheese) = N(cheese) / N(*)

Next ... word pairs are very deceptive, because they look easy, and almost
everyone does them wrong. kind-of-ish.  Does them misleadingly.  First let
me do it the misleading way, then do it the right way, and then you will
see what the generalization is.

The wrong way, for "wisconsin cheese" :  compute

P(*, cheese) = N(*, cheese) / N(*,*)

P(wisconsin, *) = N(wisconsin, *) / N(*,*)

P(wisconsin, cheese) = N(wisconsin, cheese)/ N(*,*)

 [...]

--- Message 27/28 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-06-01

Linas,

This is what we've been calling the "surprisingness" ....  Actually
the issues you mention are largely the same ones Shujing dealt with
for calculating surprisingness inside her OpenCog pattern miner...

Surprisingness contains surprises; is this surprising? ;p

Sure....  we'll think more about the best API for use here...

ben

--- Message 28/28 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-05-31

Double-check your notes and formulas.  The logarithm of the above is the
mutual information.  Its what Rohit just graphed, in his email thread. His
graph looks "normal" BTW.  Its got some noise and artifacts, but for the
high-statistics regions, it looks quite normal (I've explored word pairs
before).

OK, so I am looking at my notes now, and that email glossed over Bayes law.
Attached is a 2-page PDF extract of my notes, these handle the conditional
probabilities correctly, instead of in a hand-wavey manner; I think this
will make people like Nil happier; he likes conditional probabilities.

-- linas

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Redux, was Re Variables, names, etc. (151f9fe6adbe5276)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Redux, was Re Variables, names, etc.
Messages: 9
Date range: 2015-12-31 to 2016-01-03
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 151f9fe6adbe5276

============================================================

--- Message 1/9 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-12-31

So,

I see that Amen is checking in new code to create variables with random
names ... and I thought I would revisit this conversation.  However, I
cannot figure out what the "actual problem" is.   Can someone describe it,
concretely?  All this talk about random variable names just feels like a
hacky work-around for some ill-defined problem that is not being handled
correctly.  But I can't quite tell what the problem is.

-- linas

--- Message 2/9 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-12-31

Specifically this:

https://github.com/opencog/atomspace/tree/master/opencog/rule-engine#things-need-to-be-implemented

has two pronouncements. pronouncement (ii) seems fine and can be
implemented in the atomspace.   The need for  pronouncement (i) is what I
don't understand.

I can sit down and implement (ii) in fairly short order.

--linas

On Thu, Dec 31, 2015 at 3:42 PM, Linas Vepstas <linasvepstas@gmail.com>
wrote:

--- Message 3/9 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2016-01-01

Linas,

I guess you are referring to

***

   1. all usage of VariableNode are unique & well-defined, in that the same
   named VariableNode is never declared and appears in more than one scope

***

I think this was written by William Ma (who is on an indefinite break from
the project at the moment), based on his work with the backward chainer...

He was getting annoyed and tangled up with cases where, say,

VariableNode "X"

was used with multiple different meanings in the Atomspace, e.g.

ForAllLink
   VariableNode "X"
    ImplicationLink <.6>
           Inheritance (VariableNode "X") (ConceptNode "human")
           Inheritance (VariableNode "X") (ConceptNode "silly")

and

ForAllLink
   VariableNode "X", "Y"
    ImplicationLink <.8>
...

--- Message 4/9 ---
From: Russell Wallace
Date: 2016-01-01

For what it's worth, when I ran into that problem, I ended up going with a
different solution:

3) let autogenerated variables be anonymous as far as internal computation
is concerned; when printing out terms, if an anonymous variable is
...

--- Message 5/9 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2016-01-02

Hmm.

OK.  I think there are now tools to solve all of this, they just have to be
used.  So: the ForAllLink is derived from the ScopeLink, and you can just
ask it about what variables it might contain.  At any rate, it should not
matter what the variable names are anyway, so I don't quite get the
problem.  The actual names of the variables should not have any practical
effect on anything.

I guess I will have to dig into the backward chainer myself and see what's
going on.  I'm just kind of annoyed that there are all these "generate a
random name" utilities creeping into the code base, they strike me as being
hacky, trying to avoid addressing the actual issue.   Which I don't
understand...

--linas

--- Message 6/9 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2016-01-02

Hi Russel,

Yes, approach 3 is what got taken -- there are now little utilities that
auto-generate variable names, and my gut instinct is that its a hack
because the core design issue is not being dealt with.  But I don't quite
get it so I dunno. Just going on instinct.

--linas

On Fri, Jan 1, 2016 at 10:02 AM, Russell Wallace <russell.wallace@gmail.com>
wrote:

--- Message 7/9 ---
From: 'Nil Geisweiller' via opencog
Date: 2016-01-03

Solution 3) Use higher order operators so that you never need to 
manipulate unscoped variables. That is what I'm doing here

https://github.com/opencog/opencog/tree/master/examples/moses-pln-synergy/scm

Nil

--- Message 8/9 ---
From: 'Nil Geisweiller' via opencog
Date: 2016-01-03

I don't think (i) should be a requirement.

I can see that :-)

https://github.com/opencog/atomspace/pull/562

Nil

--- Message 9/9 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2016-01-03

On Sun, Jan 3, 2016 at 7:52 AM, 'Nil Geisweiller' via opencog <
opencog@googlegroups.com> wrote:

Yes, I agree (well, for scoped variables) -- the chainers should never need
to know the name of a scoped variable or worry about it in any way.

Ah yes, although last time we talked about this there was some issue about
the semantics of "distributing the lambda".   But I'm more worried about
the C++ internals of the chainers, than the rule forms, just right now.

--linas

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Regarding GSoC 2015 projects (14c0a4ac5b646ecb)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Regarding GSoC 2015 projects
Messages: 23
Date range: 11 Mar 2015 to 2015-06-05
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14c0a4ac5b646ecb

============================================================

--- Message 1/23 ---
From: Ainish Dave
Date: 11 Mar 2015

Hello,

I saw the GSoC 2015 project ideas page of opencog. As per my interest, I 
was redirected to the NLP -> Unsupervised Language Learning page. Under 
that page, I found a project topic related to 'morphology'. I would like to 
contribute to that. [...]

--- Message 2/23 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-03-11

What languages are you fluent in, can read and write, besides English?
Working on morphology will require being able to look at what is being
generated, and verifying that it is correct.

-- Linas

--- Message 3/23 ---
From: Ainish Dave
Date: 11 Mar 2015

Thanks for the reply Linas. Apart from English I can read and write (1) 
Hindi, (2) Gujarati. And yes, I understood that thing about morphology. So 
is there anything in particular you can provide me to work upon ?

--Ainish Dave

--- Message 4/23 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-03-12

OK!

The wiki page http://wiki.opencog.org/w/Language_learning gives the
overview  It would be useful for you to search for the emails with Rohit
Shinde for the last month or two, on this mailing list, and look them
over.  Some of the steps are similar. I am cc'ing Rohit.

I don't know of any easy way of explaining the general task, except by
giving the detailed steps.

The rough proceedural outline is this:  download a bunch of Hindi or
Gujarati. text. Wikipedia is good.  There is a collection of scripts here:
https://github.com/opencog/relex/tree/master/src/perl  the cleanup
wikipedia dumps. fr for french, en for english, pl for  polish -- you would
need to create one for hindi, one for Gujarati  This cleaned up text is
then sent though link--grammar, using the AMY langauge (not the ANY
 [...]

--- Message 5/23 ---
From: Ainish Dave
Date: 13 Mar 2015

Great! I will go through the links and mails. I shall get the detailed idea 
about things from that, so that I can start working on it. I will be 
updating here about the progress or any sort of doubts.

Thanks.

Regards,
Ainish Dave

--- Message 6/23 ---
From: Ainish Dave
Date: 17 Mar 2015

I started working as per instructed in previous mail. I downloaded Gujarati 
text, but I started working with English text only to get some idea about 
the whole procedure. It went well till cleaning up of text. Then I couldn't 
get along with that link-grammar part. [...]

--- Message 7/23 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-03-17

Oh,

yes, darn ..  I completely forget ... at this time, to make the AMY
langauge work, you also have to compile link-grammar in a special way.
Before compileing it, you have to say ..configure --enable-anysplit and
then recompile.

This compile-time flag should be made into a runtime-flag, I guess.  It is
a very special-purpose thing ...

--linas

--- Message 8/23 ---
From: Ainish Dave
Date: 7 May 2015

I have started reading theory of link-grammar and also about the morfessor 
as we discussed. 

And what should be the plan for the practical implementation? Should I 
setup whole opencog code into my system? If not, then which modules shall I 
deploy in order to work for my project?

Regards,
Ainish

--- Message 9/23 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-05-08

Hi Ainish,

Be sure to read about mutual information as well.  We will explicitly NOT
be implementing the morfessor algorithm; think of it more like a proof that
it can be done;  I want to do it a different way, by minimizing mutual
information to discover independent pieces.  Attached is a tiny worked
example of how this might go

The practical implementation should be within opencog: you need to install
cogutils, the atomspace, and then opencog itself.  There are scripts,
written mostly in scheme, that already get things started. A lot of people
don't like/have trouble with scheme, so python is another option, but
python is not as strong or as flexible; its harder to use in the long run,
although it seems easy at first.  You almost surely do not want to code in
C++ for this project.

 [. [...]

--- Message 10/23 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-05-08

Ainish,

You will also want to talk to Rohit; Rohit is another GSOC student, working
on automated syntax discovery for the English language.  Much or most of
the infrastructure is the same, and Rohit has already set things up into a
functioning system, and so may be able to help you.

Rohit --

Ainish is another GSOC student, but he will be working on automated
morphology discovery for Hindi and Gujarati. He is only now getting
started.  If you can help him get through the confusing parts, that would
be great.

I hope you two can work together: many parts of the system are in common
between the two projects, and eventually (not this year) I hope that they
merge together (since morphology discovery is a pre-requisite to syntax
 [...]

--- Message 11/23 ---
From: Rohit Shinde
Date: 2015-05-09

Linas,
Yes, we certainly can work together.

Ainish,
I would be happy to help you out with whatever problem you have, since I
have the same system set up.

On Fri, May 8, 2015 at 10:27 PM, Linas Vepstas <linasvepstas@gmail.com>
wrote:

--- Message 12/23 ---
From: Ainish Dave
Date: 8 May 2015

Linas,

Yes I will keep in mind those things. And me & Rohit will surely work 
together for the common part, for better results. Thanks. I will update you 
about my further understandings and doubts.

Rohit,
Thanks. I saw that you have got your system set up and working great. I am 
 [...]

--- Message 13/23 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-05-09

Ainish, Rohit, it might be easier to subscribe to he link-grammar mailing
list instead, and post there: the email traffic is much much lower, and
thus, less likely to overwhelm you.  You are welcome to CC this list; it
just might be easier over there.

-- Linas

--- Message 14/23 ---
From: Rohit Shinde
Date: 2015-05-10

Yes, if you look at this thread -->
https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/opencog/8HWcT2kKxX0
This is the thread where I asked most of my questions. The earlier mails in
this thread are all about me setting my system up. So you can go through
them. You can ask me if you have any problems.

--- Message 15/23 ---
From: Aaron Nitzkin
Date: 2015-05-11

I imagine it might be helpful for Gavriel to be in on these discussions as
well.  He is just now about to try to install everything he needs to play
around with the language pipeline, and I think the better he understands
linkgrammar and the advantages and disadvantages of applying it in the
 [...]

--- Message 16/23 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-05-11

Aaron, I kind of wish you would stop saying the provocative, inflammatory
things that you say.  We've been round the table on this. The onus is on
you to provide some real-world examples to back your claims.

Right now, linkgrammar is the #1 state-of-the-art English parser out there;
there is none better. I do not want to confuse either Rohit or Ainish about
this: they have very specific tasks, I don't want to replace that with
vague blurbling about limitations and replacements.

--linas

--- Message 17/23 ---
From: Aaron Nitzkin
Date: 2015-05-12

Linas there is nothing provocative or inflammatory or anything I need to
prove about mentioning that a piece of software has
"advantages and disadvantages" and "limitations."   Link grammar is a
wonderful piece of software, and I am happy to take your
 [...]

--- Message 18/23 ---
From: Ainish Dave
Date: 20 May 2015

Hi Linas,

I updated the current wiki-clean-* file to use it for Gujarati and Hindi 
languages.

Now the question is, do I need to sort them alphabetically in directories? 
Is it mandatory? Because Guajrati has 35 letters and its 12 variants a 
piece. So, there needs to be 420 directories. [...]

--- Message 19/23 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-05-20

excellent.  You should push them out to the git repo, and submit a pull
request.

Absolutely not.  I split the English articles into letters, because there
were millions of them, and I ran parsing on multiple machines, each machine
doing one letter.

You can put them all into one directory, or split in any convenient manner,
if it seems there are too many for just one directory.

I would hope so.

To get reasonable statistical results, each word has to be observed at
least dozens,  maybe hundreds of times or more. For common words, a few
hundred articles will suffice.  For rare and uncommon words, probably many
more.  At any rate, the initial exploration will be of common words, so
this should not be a problem.  (Although I guess that common words tend to
 [...]

--- Message 20/23 ---
From: Ainish Dave
Date: 27 May 2015

Hi Linas,

How do I connect opencog to postgres? Rohit gave me the readme file, of the 
time before the project was restructured. And currently, there are no 
directories in 'persist' folder which needs to be there as instructed in 
that readme file.

--Ainish

--- Message 21/23 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-05-28

They are all in the  https://github.com/opencog/atomspace project.

--linas

--- Message 22/23 ---
From: Ainish Dave
Date: 2015-06-01

I am getting these errors while building link-grammar and connecting
opencog to database respectively:

*1) *while building link-grammar, I used ' ./configure --enable-anysplit '
as you suggested quite a while back. But, after that while making, it gives
the following error.

../link-grammar/. [...]

--- Message 23/23 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-06-05

Hi,

try saying 'make clean; make'  that should fix it.

The setup instructions are here:
https://github.com/opencog/atomspace/tree/master/opencog/persist/sql  --
the instructions start about 1/3rd of the way down.  Make sure that you
haven't omitted any f the steps.

I have a server that you could use -- that might make things easier. This
project requires you to process a lot of text -- cpu-weeks or cpu-months
worth -- so if you don't have a strong, reliable system to do this on, then
it will be hard.

--linas

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Regular Meetup Architectural Tutorial Google Hangouts (14d0c8df93e1016f)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Regular Meetup Architectural Tutorial Google Hangouts
Messages: 3
Date range: 30 Apr 2015 to 2015-05-02
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14d0c8df93e1016f

============================================================

--- Message 1/3 ---
From: Curtis M. Faith
Date: 30 Apr 2015

I'm going to organize a regular series of architectural tutorial sessions: 1) Hangout with newbs, 2) Writeup of tutorials and whitepaper docs, 3) Ongoing discussions for key architectural decisions, 4) Weekly summaries of changes to docs on our blog.

--- Message 2/3 ---
From: Mahesh Kommareddi
Date: 2015-04-30

Hi, I just joined the group out of an interest to get involved. I hope these sessions will be open to others besides those coming through GSoC.

--- Message 3/3 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-05-02

Curtis,

This sounds awesome and I'm glad you're taking the initiative here...!! ;)

I just have one comment: I think it is great for folks to have discussion of key architectural decisions in tutorial sessions... But of course, we can't expect decisions about key architectural decisions to be made in these sessions -- for one thing because everyone who needs to be involved won't be in the sessions.

-- ben
